REGIONAL OFFICE (RO)

Relatively large VA field office that generally includes staff from all of the VBA elements - Director's Office, Customer Service Center (Adjudication and Veterans Services), Loan Guaranty, Vocational Rehabilitation and Counseling, and Support Services.

REGIONAL PROCESSING OFFICE (RPO)

VA offices that process Education claims. Currently, there are two RPOs – Buffalo, NY (RO 307) and Muskogee, OK (RO 351).

RELEASED FROM ACTIVE DUTY (RAD)

Date a Veteran is released from active duty.

Overview

This section discusses how to review the payment history of chapter 33 claims.

In This Section

This section contains the following topics:

Topic

Topic Name

Topic 1: Reviewing BTs Issued on or after July 8, 2024

Topic 2: Reviewing BTs Issued before July 8, 2024

Topic 1: Reviewing BTs Issued on or after July 8, 2024

Introduction

This section covers viewing payment records in the Enterprise Management of Payment Workload and Reporting (eMPWR) after the transition from BDN for Chapter 33 processing.

Change Date

June 24, 2025

II.2.E.1.a

General

Users who review Education Benefit payments under the Chapter 33 for payments issued on or after July 8, 2024, will do so using the eMPWR user interface in SalesForce.  For details on logging on and accessing eMPWR in SalesForce, please review the Logging into the Application section of the eMPWR User Guide.

Unless additional access is specifically needed for their position or role, individuals will be given access to the eMPWR system with an inquiry (read-only) role. Any fiscal transactions needed for the correction or completion of a Chapter 33 record must be routed for review and processing by the Regional Processing Office’s (RPO) Support Services Division (SSD).

Users can search for a participant (beneficiary) by SSN or file number.

After a successful participant search is conducted from the home screen, the participant record is displayed via Participant Record screen with tabs including Transaction History, Participant Details, Notes, and eFolder.
   

Figure: eMPWR Participant Search
 

Figure: eMPWR Participant Record screen

Note: Due to changes in how relationships are established for TOE beneficiaries, when payments need to be reviewed for a TOE beneficiary who was paid via both BDN and eMPWR, multiple File Number searches will be needed – one using the transferee’s file number (for payments made via BDN) and one using the transferor’s file number (for payments made via eMPWR).

II.2.E.1.b

Transaction History Tab

When processing a claim, responding to questions received via the telephone or written correspondence, Education employees require the ability to search the system to retrieve all transactions associated with the searched participant. The Transaction History tab contains the following information.

1.    All Transactions – displays all transaction history records, not just those transactions sent from Education service.

2.    Deductions – displays active Benefit Repayment and Offsets deduction records. The processor can change the default filter to display inactive deductions.

3.    Payments – displays all payments scheduled for release by treasury and the details of those payment records.

4.    Receivables – displays all active receivable records with a balance greater than zero. The processor can change the default filter to display inactive receivables.

After a successful participant search, the system redirects to the Transaction History tab on the Participant Record page, displaying the All Transactions default grid for Accountable Balances.

Figure: eMPWR Transaction History – All Transactions example

 

Figure: eMPWR Transaction History – Payments example

Figure: eMPWR Transaction History – Receivables example

From this view, the processor also can view the details of a transaction by clicking on the Transaction Id.  Details vary depending upon the transaction but can include the following.

1.    Account Details/Balance Summary (Receivables, Deductions, Accountable Balances)

a.    Status of the Transaction, including outstanding balances (if applicable)

2.    Payment Details (Payment transactions only)

3.    Notes (All transaction types)

a.      Any notes entered during transaction processing.

4.    Historical Transactions against the account (Receivables, Deductions, Accountable Balances)

a.      Any transactions made against the account.

5.    Associated Documents (Accountable Balances)

a.      If any documents were uploaded as part of establishing the account/transaction

6.    Payment Address (Payment transactions only)

7.    Details about payment address or EFT account.

The processor can select the gear icon to export the transaction details to Microsoft Excel CSV format.

  Figure: eMPWR Transaction History – Export

Select the Close button to exit the window and return to the Transaction History grid.

Go to Top

Topic 2: Reviewing BTs Issued before July 8, 2024

Introduction

This section covers the process to reviewing transactions released before the transition from BDN to eMPWR.

Change Date

June 24, 2025

II.2.E.2.a

General

While Veterans Claims Examiners (VCEs) can no longer establish (CEST) or adjust (CADJ) Chapter 33 Master Records in BDN upon the transition to eMPWR, VCEs still can view the master record and transaction history from before the conversion in BDN.  To do so, users will follow the processes found in M22-4, Part XII, 9.02 - 9.06. 

Note: All active account receivables have been migrated to eMPWR as part of the conversion.  As such, no information will display on the M01 Accounts Receivable screen.

Note: No transactions submitted on or after July 8, 2024, will display in BDN.  VCEs must review eMPWR to view these transactions. This means that both eMPWR and BDN will need to be reviewed if a beneficiary has activity both before and after July 8, 2024.

Go to Top

retrieved_chunks.title: Sub-Chapter 5. Distance Learning, Independent Study, and Internet Training
heading_path: Sub-Chapter 5. Distance Learning, Independent Study, and Internet Training
retrieved_chunks.content:
Any courses that consist of interaction using communications technology and/or some standard class sessions, but do not meet the requirements of in-residence training, are considered independent study or distance learning.

b. Benefit Payment

Distance learning, independent study, and Internet training are generally offered by Institutions of Higher Learning (such as colleges and universities), and similar rules and rates apply for tuition and fees, monthly housing allowance, books and supplies stipend, and Yellow Ribbon benefits.

c. Housing Allowance

The rules for housing benefits for veterans enrolled in independent training, distance learning, or Internet training are slightly different from the rules for veterans at IHLs.

Under the Post-9/11 GI Bill, the individual must be enrolled in at least one “in-residence” course to receive the full monthly housing allowance based on the Department of Defense’s (DoD’s) Basic Allowance for Housing (BAH) rate for the school’s ZIP Code.

As of October 1, 2011, individuals are eligible to receive a monthly housing allowance based on one-half of the national average of DoD’s BAH rates if enrolled solely in distance learning, independent training, or Internet training.

Note: As for the other training programs, monthly housing allowance eligibility requires a Rate of Pursuit (RoP) of more than .50, and benefits are pro-rated based on the RoP and the individual’s benefit level.

Sources

Electronic Code of Federal Regulations, § 21.9590 Approved programs of education and courses.

Electronic Code of Federal Regulations, § 21.9505 Definitions.

Policy Advisory: Independent Study/Distance Learning vs. Resident Training, March 26, 2010

PL 111-377, Post-9/11 Veterans Educational Assistance Improvements Act of 2010, Title II Other Educational Assistance Matters, Section 105. Educational Assistance for Programs of Education Other than Programs of Education Leading to a Degree

retrieved_chunks.title: Part 4: Chapter 11 - Enrollment Adjustments
heading_path: 11.17  NON-CHAPTER 33 ISSUES IF TOTAL CHARGES IS A FACTOR
retrieved_chunks.content:
For non-chapter 33 benefits, if a reduction in credit hours is to less than one-half time training, the charges (tuition and fees) to be considered in determining the monthly rate will only be the charges for the reduced load, even though the school may not refund charges made for the part of the course load dropped. The charges for the reduced load will be prorated over the entire enrollment period to determine the reduced monthly rate of benefits. The reduced rate, however, is payable only from the date of reduction in the training time or from the end of the month in which the reduction in training time occurred, whichever is appropriate

EXAMPLE: A student receiving chapter 30 benefits is enrolled in training at an IHL for 15 semester hours effective January 10 through May 8. He subsequently reduced his training load to:

10 credit hours effective February 9

7 credit hours effective March 27 and

3 credit hours effective April 28

The normal school charge for tuition and fee students enrolled for 3 credit hours is $120. The first reduction occurred during the school's established drop period. The second reduction was due to acceptable mitigating circumstances. The third reduction will result in a punitive grade. The VCE should authorize payment as follows:

‘‘(1) In the case of an individual entitled to educational assistance under this chapter by reason of section 3311(b)(1) or 3311(b)(2), amounts as follows:

‘‘(A) An amount equal to the established charges for the program of education, except that the amount payable under this subparagraph may not exceed the maximum amount of established charges regularly charged in-State students for full-time pursuit of approved programs of education for undergraduates by the public institution of higher education offering approved programs of education for undergraduates in the State in which the individual is enrolled that has the highest rate of regularly-charged established charges for such programs of education among all public institutions of higher education in such State offering such programs of education.

‘‘(B) A monthly stipend in an amount as follows:

‘‘(i) For each month the individual pursues the program of education (other than, in the case of assistance under this section only, a program of education offered through distance learning), a monthly housing stipend amount equal to the monthly amount of the basic allowance for housing payable under section 403 of title 37 for a member with dependents in pay grade E–5 residing in the military housing area that encompasses all or the majority portion of the ZIP code area in which is located the institution of higher education at which the individual is enrolled.

‘‘(ii) For the first month of each quarter, semester, or term, as applicable, of the program of education pursued by the individual, a lump sum amount for books, supplies, equipment, and other educational costs with respect to such quarter, semester, or term in the amount equal to—

‘‘(I) $1,000, multiplied by

‘‘(II) the fraction which is the portion of a complete academic year under the program of education that such quarter, semester, or term constitutes.

‘‘(2) In the case of an individual entitled to educational assistance under this chapter by reason of section 3311(b)(3), amounts equal to 90 percent of the amounts that would be payable to the individual under paragraph (1) for the program of education if the individual were entitled to amounts for the program of education under paragraph (1) rather than this paragraph.

‘‘(3) In the case of an individual entitled to educational assistance under this chapter by reason of section 3311(b)(4), amounts equal to 80 percent of the amounts that would be payable to the individual under paragraph (1) for the program of education if the individual were entitled to amounts for the program of education under paragraph (1) rather than this paragraph.

‘‘(4) In the case of an individual entitled to educational assistance under this chapter by reason of section 3311(b)(5), amounts equal to 70 percent of the amounts that would be payable to the individual under paragraph (1) for the program of education if the individual were entitled to amounts for the program of education

[Chunk 2] Source: 554400000073789 — Sub-Chapter 5. Distance Learning, Independent Study, and Internet Training Heading: Sub-Chapter 5. Distance Learning, Independent Study, and Internet Training

Any courses that consist of interaction using communications technology and/or some standard class sessions, but do not meet the requirements of in-residence training, are considered independent study or distance learning.

b. Benefit Payment

Distance learning, independent study, and Internet training are generally offered by Institutions of Higher Learning (such as colleges and universities), and similar rules and rates apply for tuition and fees, monthly housing allowance, books and supplies stipend, and Yellow Ribbon benefits.

c. Housing Allowance

The rules for housing benefits for veterans enrolled in independent training, distance learning, or Internet training are slightly different from the rules for veterans at IHLs.

Under the Post-9/11 GI Bill, the individual must be enrolled in at least one “in-residence” course to receive the full monthly housing allowance based on the Department of Defense’s (DoD’s) Basic Allowance for Housing (BAH) rate for the school’s ZIP Code.

As of October 1, 2011, individuals are eligible to receive a monthly housing allowance based on one-half of the national average of DoD’s BAH rates if enrolled solely in distance learning, independent training, or Internet training.

Note: As for the other training programs, monthly housing allowance eligibility requires a Rate of Pursuit (RoP) of more than .50, and benefits are pro-rated based on the RoP and the individual’s benefit level.

## MHA Rate for Distance Learning Only (Chapter 33)

Under the Post-9/11 GI Bill (Chapter 33), a veteran taking **only** distance learning courses receives a Monthly Housing Allowance (MHA) equal to **50% (one-half) of the national average of the DoD's Basic Allowance for Housing (BAH) rates** payable in the U.S.

This rate has been in effect since **October 1, 2011**. [1][2][3]

### Key Conditions:
- This reduced rate applies when the individual is enrolled **solely** in distance learning, independent training, or Internet training [2]
- To receive **full** MHA based on the school's ZIP code BAH rate, the veteran must be enrolled in **at least one "in-residence" course** [2]
- Monthly housing allowance eligibility still requires a **Rate of Pursuit (RoP) of more than .50**, and benefits are pro-rated based on the RoP and the individual's benefit level [2]

SUBCHAPTER III, MEASURING ENROLLMENTS WITH SPECIAL TYPES OF COURSES
6.12 How to Measure Overlapping Courses Offered in Both Credit and Clock Hours
retrieved_chunks.content:
a. Background

In some cases, a student is enrolled in both credit hour and clock hour courses that are overlapping.

Reference:  38 CFR 21.7172

b.  Principle 1

If a clock-hour  course overlaps with a course taken at the primary school that is measured on a semester- or quarter-hour basis, convert the clock hours to equivalent semester or quarter hours.  (See par. 1.09a to determine which school is the primary school.)  To make this conversion:

  Divide the number of semester or quarter hours required for full-time attendance at the primary school by the number of clock hours required for full-time attendance at the secondary school.

   Multiply the result by the number of clock hours in which the student is enrolled.

  Drop any fraction that my result.

c.  Example 1

A student is taking 9 semester hours concurrently with 6 clock hours.  Twelve semester hours is full time at the primary school and 25 clock hours is full time at the secondary school.

Computation:  12 credit hours/25 clock hours  = .48;

                       6 clock hours X .48 = 2.88 equivalent semester hours. 

                       Then 9 + 2.88 = 11 equivalent semester hours.  (Fractions at the final step only are dropped.)

Authorize 3/4 time benefits based on 11 equivalent semester hours.

d.  Principle 2

If a semester- or quarter-hour course overlaps with a course taken at the primary school measured on a clock-hour basis, convert the semester or quarter hours to equivalent clock hours.  If the semester or quarter hours are for nonstandard terms, first convert credit hours to equivalent credit hours as described in paragraph 6.06.  To convert semester or quarter hours to equivalent credit hours:

  Divide the number of clock hours required for full-time attendance at the primary school by the number of semester or quarters hours required for full time at the secondary school.

  Multiply the result by the number of semester or quarter hours in which the student is enrolled.

  Drop any fraction that may result.

e.  Example 2

A student is taking 9 clock hours and 3 semester hours in a regular semester.

(18 clock hours is considered full time and 12 semester hours is full time.)

Computation:  18 clock hours/12 semester hours  = 1.5.

                       3 semester hours X 1.5 = 4.5 clock hours.

                       Then, 9 + 4.5 = 13 clock hours.  (Fractions at the final step only are dropped.)

Authorize benefits based on 13 clock hours.

results.answer:
## MHA Calculation for a Part-Time Student (9 Credits, 12 Credit Full-Time)

Based on the provided policy guidance, here is how MHA is calculated:

### Step 1: Determine Rate of Pursuit (RoP)
Divide the enrolled credits by the full-time credit threshold:
> **9 ÷ 12 = 0.75 (Rate of Pursuit)**

### Step 2: Multiply BAH Rate by Rate of Pursuit
> **MHA = BAH Rate × 0.75**

The result is then **rounded to the nearest multiple of 10**. [1]

### Step 3: Confirm Percentage of BAH
Per the enrollment chart, a student enrolled in **9 credits** (RoP of 0.75) receives **80% of the applicable BAH rate**. [2]

> ⚠️ **Note:** Although the RoP is 0.75 (75%), the rounding rule results in **80%** of BAH being payable, as shown in the chart.

---

**Policy Effective Date:** This prorated MHA calculation has been in effect since **October 1, 2011**. [1]

[1][2]

retrieved_chunks.title: Part 3: Chapter 4 - Delimiting Date Extensions
heading_path: 4.02 EXTENSIONS BASED ON A LATER PERIOD OF ACTIVE DUTY
retrieved_chunks.content:
a. General. Typically, individuals eligible under Chapters 30, 32 or Section 903 may receive education benefits for a period not to exceed 10 years from date of discharge; however, a later period of qualifying active duty service may give the individual a new 10-year delimiting period for these benefits.  Veterans Claims Examiners (VCEs) should verify this service in the same manner as any other period of service.

Typically, individuals eligible under Chapter 33 may receive education benefits for a period not to exceed 15 years from date of discharge; however, a later qualifying period of active duty service may give the individual a new 15-year delimiting period for this benefit. The period of eligibility for Chapter 33 is based on the individual’s last date of discharge from any qualifying period.  VCEs should verify this service in the same manner as any other period of service.

NOTE: If the individual’s date of discharge is after January 1, 2013, the individual will have no delimiting date under Chapter 33.

b. Length of Later Period of Active Duty. For purposes of giving an individual a new period of eligibility (and a new delimiting date), a later period of active duty must be at least 90 days in length if it ended on or after December 18, 1989. The only exceptions to this 90-day requirement are for individuals who are discharged or released due to a service-connected disability, for a non-service connected medical condition which pre-existed the later active duty service, for hardship, or because of a Reduction in Force (RIF) for convenience of the government. (38 Code of Federal Regulations (CFR) 21.7050(a), 21.5041(a), 21.5745(a)) Under Chapter 33, only a service-connected disablity discharge may be for a period less than 90 continious days. (38 USC 3321)

NOTE:VA’s General Counsel determined VA is not extending the delimiting date, but actually granting a new eligibility period beginning on the date of the claimant’s last release from active duty. Consequently, if the member re-enters active duty after his original delimiting date has passed, that member would not qualify for benefits until their 90th day back on active duty. The new 10-year eligibility period would begin once the claimant is released from active duty for the subsequent period.

3.11  SPECIFIC ISSUES REGARDING VERIFICATION OF SERVICE
b. Full-time Duty in the Reserve or National Guard.
retrieved_chunks.content:
In some instances full-time duty in the Reserve or National Guard may be considered active duty under Title 38. For Chapter 30, see Part 5 Chapter 1 - General . National Guard members and Reservists who have served a minimum of 90 days of qualifying service are eligible to received Chapter 33 benefits. For additional information regarding Chapter 33 benefits, refer to  Sub-Chapter 2. Veterans paragraph 2.01(c).

(1) Duty Status of Reservists. At the beginning of an enlistment in the Reserves, an individual is required to perform Initial Active Duty for Training (IADT) for a period of not less than 4 months. The remainder of the enlistment is spent as a member of the Ready Reserves.

A. Active or Inactive Duty for Training. Title 38 CFR 3.6 describes active and inactive duty for training. If a Reservist's only service is active or inactive duty for training, the Reservist must have died or sustained a disability during the period of training to be considered a Veteran. If the period of training did not result in death or disability, the Reservist is not a Veteran based on that service.

B. Full-time Duty in the Uniformed Services. Since the 1960's the Reserve components have had several programs such as the Active Guard Reserve (AGR) and the Active Duty Support (ADS) Program in which members serve full-time in operational or support positions but are never formally called to active duty. This type of service, whether it lasts one day or 3 years, is classified by the service departments as "active duty for training (ACDUTRA)."

The term "full-time duty in the uniformed services" is not defined in 38 U.S.C or 38 CFR. However, an opinion issued by the General Counsel on November 9, 1988, makes it clear that, despite the military's ACDUTRA classification, VA has the authority to declare certain types of service performed by Reservists to be "active duty" for the purposes of VA benefits. According to this opinion, Reservists meet the definition of active duty if the facts of record establish that the service was full-time and was for operational or support (as opposed to training) purposes.

Certain types of Reservist duty are generally NOT "full-time duty in the armed forces." These include the following:

(a) IADT (Except for Chapter 33 depending upon total aggregate service)

retrieved_chunks.title: Sub-Chapter 2. Veterans
retrieved_chunks.content:
a. Veterans are eligible for Post-9/11 GI Bill Education Benefits if they served a minimum of 90 days on active duty after September 10, 2001, or at least 30 days if discharged for a service-connected disability or sole survivorship. This covers certain types of active duty served as a member of the Uniformed Services or because of a call up or mobilization to active duty from a reserve component (National Guard or Selected Reserve) under certain sections of title 10, title 14, or title 32.

Basic entry-level and skills training does not count towards qualifying service unless the Veteran has completed 730 days (24 months) of service. For more information, see 70/80 rule below.

Note: 38 USC § 3301 and 38 CFR § 21.9505 specifically use the term “Armed Forces” when referencing qualifying active duty service, rather than “Uniformed Services”. VA General Counsel has determined the two terms have the same meaning for purposes of VA benefits due to 33 USC 3072(a) and 42 USC 213(d), stating service in the National Oceanic and Atmospheric Administration and Public Health Service, respectively, is considered active military service under laws administered by the Secretary of Veterans Affairs. Therefore, the reference to “Armed Forces” in 38 USC § 3301 should be treated as having the same meaning as “Uniformed Services”.

b. Veterans

To qualify for chapter 33 benefits, a Veteran must have served at least 90 aggregate (total) days of active duty service after September 10, 2001, and have an honorable discharge. Three years of total service or a discharge due to a service-connected disability or sole survivorship will qualify a Veteran for 100% of the Post-9/11 GI Bill benefit. Less than 3 years of cumulative service will qualify a Veteran for a lower percentage of benefits.

c. National Guard/Reserve

National Guard members and reservists who have served a minimum of 90 days of qualifying service are eligible to receive chapter 33 benefits.

Note: Effective October 1, 2011, VA began paying benefits to members of the Army National Guard and Air National Guard based on qualifying service under title 32. Payments may be made retroactively for enrollment(s) at an approved educational institution for attendance on or after August 1, 2009. The following service may be qualifying:

heading_path: 2.02 Length of Service and Separation Reasons2.02 Length of Service and Separation Reasons
retrieved_chunks.content:
*The 70/80% rule is as follows:

If the aggregate service including training is at least 24 months (730-909 days) but less than 30 months (910-1094 days) AND the aggregate service excluding training is at least 18 months (545 to 729 days) but less than 24 months, the individual will be deemed eligible at the 70% benefit level. See Sub-Chapter 5 Eligibility Excluded Service.

Note: Historically, a 40% benefit level existed for individuals with at least 90 days, but less than 6 months of service. The 40% benefit level was eliminated, effective August 1, 2020, and became the 50% benefit level. In a corresponding change, the previous service requirements for 50% benefit level (at least 6 months but less than 12 months) and 60% benefit level (at least 12 months but less than 18 months) were consolidated, with the service requirement for 60% benefit level becoming at least 6 months but less than 18 months. Individuals previously eligible at the 40% benefit level are eligible to receive payment at the 50% benefit level for enrollments beginning on or after August 1, 2020.

Full-time Duty in the Reserve or National Guard.

In some instances full-time duty in the Reserve or National Guard may be considered active duty under Title 38. For Chapter 30, see Part 5 Chapter 1 - General . National Guard members and Reservists who have served a minimum of 90 days of qualifying service are eligible to received Chapter 33 benefits. (1) Duty Status of Reservists. At the beginning of an enlistment in the Reserves, an individual is required to perform Initial Active Duty for Training (IADT) for a period of not less than 4 months. The remainder of the enlistment is spent as a member of the Ready Reserves.

A. Active or Inactive Duty for Training. Title 38 CFR 3.6 describes active and inactive duty for training. If a Reservist's only service is active or inactive duty for training, the Reservist must have died or sustained a disability during the period of training to be considered a Veteran. If the period of training did not result in death or disability, the Reservist is not a Veteran based on that service.

B. Full-time Duty in the Uniformed Services. Since the 1960's the Reserve components have had several programs such as the Active Guard Reserve (AGR) and the Active Duty Support (ADS) Program in which members serve full-time in operational or support positions but are never formally called to active duty. This type of service, whether it lasts one day or 3 years, is classified by the service departments as "active duty for training (ACDUTRA)."

The term "full-time duty in the uniformed services" is not defined in 38 U.S.C or 38 CFR. However, an opinion issued by the General Counsel on November 9, 1988, makes it clear that, despite the military's ACDUTRA classification, VA has the authority to declare certain types of service performed by Reservists to be "active duty" for the purposes of VA benefits. According to this opinion, Reservists meet the definition of active duty if the facts of record establish that the service was full-time and was for operational or support (as opposed to training) purposes.

a. Veterans are eligible for Post-9/11 GI Bill Education Benefits if they served a minimum of 90 days on active duty after September 10, 2001, or at least 30 days if discharged for a service-connected disability or sole survivorship. This covers certain types of active duty served as a member of the Uniformed Services or because of a call up or mobilization to active duty from a reserve component (National Guard or Selected Reserve) under certain sections of title 10, title 14, or title 32.

Basic entry-level and skills training does not count towards qualifying service unless the Veteran has completed 730 days (24 months) of service. For more information, see 70/80 rule below.

Note: 38 USC § 3301 and 38 CFR § 21.9505 specifically use the term “Armed Forces” when referencing qualifying active duty service, rather than “Uniformed Services”. VA General Counsel has determined the two terms have the same meaning for purposes of VA benefits due to 33 USC 3072(a) and 42 USC 213(d), stating service in the National Oceanic and Atmospheric Administration and Public Health Service, respectively, is considered active military service under laws administered by the Secretary of Veterans Affairs. Therefore, the reference to “Armed Forces” in 38 USC § 3301 should be treated as having the same meaning as “Uniformed Services”.

b. Veterans

To qualify for chapter 33 benefits, a Veteran must have served at least 90 aggregate (total) days of active duty service after September 10, 2001, and have an honorable discharge. Three years of total service or a discharge due to a service-connected disability or sole survivorship will qualify a Veteran for 100% of the Post-9/11 GI Bill benefit. Less than 3 years of cumulative service will qualify a Veteran for a lower percentage of benefits.

c. National Guard/Reserve

National Guard members and reservists who have served a minimum of 90 days of qualifying service are eligible to receive chapter 33 benefits.

Note: Historically, a 40% benefit level existed for individuals with at least 90 days, but less than 6 months of service. In a corresponding change, the previous service requirements for 50% benefit level (at least 6 months but less than 12 months) and 60% benefit level (at least 12 months but less than 18 months) were consolidated, with the service requirement for 60% benefit level becoming at least 6 months but less than 18 months. retrieved_chunks.title: Part 3: Chapter 4 - Delimiting Date Extensions
retrieved_chunks.content:
a. Individuals eligible under the various laws described in this manual have a period of eligibility during which they must use their entitlement. The first day after an individual's period of eligibility expires is known as his or her delimiting date.

Effective August 18, 2017, per Public Law 115-48, individuals who qualify under Chapter 33 benefits and separate from Active Duty with an Honorable discharge after January 1, 2013, do not have a period of eligibility to complete their education benefits.  These individuals do not have a delimiting date and are, therefore, not subject to delimiting date restrictions. 

b. An individual's period of eligibility may be extended under certain situations. Extensions to an individual's period of eligibility are frequently called "delimiting date extensions." Figure 4.01 provides a summary of the various types of delimiting date extensions for claimants under Chapters 33, 30, 32, 35, 1606, 1607, and Section 903. Other paragraphs in this chapter explain each type of extension.

c. Reservists receiving benefits under Chapter 1606 do not have a delimiting date if they remain actively drilling.  Once they are no longer drilling, they are no longer entitled to benefits and do not have a period of eligibility unless one of the specific situations outlined below occurs.

NOTE: In Ozer v. Principi, the United States Court of Appeals for Veterans Claims (CAVC) decided Chapter 35 surviving spouses do not have a delimiting date. Prior to the Ozer decision, both spouses and surviving spouses had a 10-year window to use their Chapter 35 benefits.  The Ozer decision was invalidated by Public Law 107-103, which reinstated the delimiting date for spouses effective December 28, 2001. Therefore, a delimiting date extension remains an issue for Chapter 35 spouses. Those affected by the Ozer decision, continue to not have a delimiting date as long as they have not changed their program.

 

 Figure 4.01. Applicability of Delimiting Date Extensions

retrieved_chunks.title: Part 3: Chapter 4 - Delimiting Date Extensions
heading_path: 4.02 EXTENSIONS BASED ON A LATER PERIOD OF ACTIVE DUTY
retrieved_chunks.content:
a. General. Typically, individuals eligible under Chapters 30, 32 or Section 903 may receive education benefits for a period not to exceed 10 years from date of discharge; however, a later period of qualifying active duty service may give the individual a new 10-year delimiting period for these benefits.  Veterans Claims Examiners (VCEs) should verify this service in the same manner as any other period of service.

Typically, individuals eligible under Chapter 33 may receive education benefits for a period not to exceed 15 years from date of discharge; however, a later qualifying period of active duty service may give the individual a new 15-year delimiting period for this benefit. The period of eligibility for Chapter 33 is based on the individual’s last date of discharge from any qualifying period.  VCEs should verify this service in the same manner as any other period of service.

NOTE: If the individual’s date of discharge is after January 1, 2013, the individual will have no delimiting date under Chapter 33.

b. Length of Later Period of Active Duty. For purposes of giving an individual a new period of eligibility (and a new delimiting date), a later period of active duty must be at least 90 days in length if it ended on or after December 18, 1989. The only exceptions to this 90-day requirement are for individuals who are discharged or released due to a service-connected disability, for a non-service connected medical condition which pre-existed the later active duty service, for hardship, or because of a Reduction in Force (RIF) for convenience of the government. (38 Code of Federal Regulations (CFR) 21.7050(a), 21.5041(a), 21.5745(a)) Under Chapter 33, only a service-connected disablity discharge may be for a period less than 90 continious days. (38 USC 3321)

NOTE:VA’s General Counsel determined VA is not extending the delimiting date, but actually granting a new eligibility period beginning on the date of the claimant’s last release from active duty. Consequently, if the member re-enters active duty after his original delimiting date has passed, that member would not qualify for benefits until their 90th day back on active duty. The new 10-year eligibility period would begin once the claimant is released from active duty for the subsequent period.

results.answer:
Based on the provided context, **Title 32 service can be qualifying** for the Post-9/11 GI Bill, but only under specific conditions.

The following Title 32 service is qualifying:

- **Full-time service in the National Guard** for the purpose of organizing, administering, recruiting, instructing, or training [3]
- **Activations in support of a national emergency under Section 502(f) of Title 32** [3]

Effective **October 1, 2011**, VA began paying benefits to members of the Army National Guard and Air National Guard based on qualifying Title 32 service, with retroactive payments possible for enrollments on or after **August 1, 2009** [1].

However, Title 32 service is **NOT qualifying** if it was called up to active duty under Title 32 but was **not** under Title 32 §502(f) for the purpose of responding to a national emergency declared by the President and supported by Federal funds [2].

> **Note:** The context does not specifically use the term "AGR" (Active Guard Reserve). results.query: Can a veteran use GI Bill benefits for an avocational or recreational course?
results.expected_answer: Avocational or recreational courses are presumed prohibited under chapters 30, 32, and 35; the course may only be paid for if there is evidence establishing that the course is of bona fide use in pursuit of the veteran's vocational objective.

retrieved_chunks.content:
(1)  The following courses are presumed to be avocational or recreational in character and require evidence establishing that the course will be of bona fide use in the pursuit of the business or occupation:

(i)  Any photography or entertainment course; 

(ii)  Any music course, instrument or vocal, public speaking course, or course in dancing, sports, or athletics, such as horseback riding, swimming, fishing, skiing, golf, baseball, tennis, bowling, sports officiating, or other sport or athletic courses, except courses of applied music, physical education, or public speaking which are offered by IHLs for credit as an integral part of a program leading to an educational objective; or

(iii)  Any other type of course which [the Education Division] determines to be avocational or recreational.

(2)  The evidence required to prove a course is not avocational or recreational can include, but is not limited to:

(i)  A statement on the application by the applicant explaining the business or occupational objective.

(ii) A statement from someone in the business or from someone who employs persons in the contemplated occupation showing the need and use of the course.  Such statements will not be required when the claimant applies for an approved correspondence course if the stated objective is the one specified in the approval notice.  In addition, when it has been established by the SAA (State Approving Agency) and the RO (Regional Office) that an approved photography course in a particular school does lead to employment as a photographer, such a statement will not be required.

heading_path: Sub-Chapter 5. Distance Learning, Independent Study, and Internet Training
retrieved_chunks.content:
Any courses that consist of interaction using communications technology and/or some standard class sessions, but do not meet the requirements of in-residence training, are considered independent study or distance learning.

b. Benefit Payment

Distance learning, independent study, and Internet training are generally offered by Institutions of Higher Learning (such as colleges and universities), and similar rules and rates apply for tuition and fees, monthly housing allowance, books and supplies stipend, and Yellow Ribbon benefits.

c. Housing Allowance

The rules for housing benefits for veterans enrolled in independent training, distance learning, or Internet training are slightly different from the rules for veterans at IHLs.

Under the Post-9/11 GI Bill, the individual must be enrolled in at least one “in-residence” course to receive the full monthly housing allowance based on the Department of Defense’s (DoD’s) Basic Allowance for Housing (BAH) rate for the school’s ZIP Code.

As of October 1, 2011, individuals are eligible to receive a monthly housing allowance based on one-half of the national average of DoD’s BAH rates if enrolled solely in distance learning, independent training, or Internet training.

Note: As for the other training programs, monthly housing allowance eligibility requires a Rate of Pursuit (RoP) of more than .50, and benefits are pro-rated based on the RoP and the individual’s benefit level.

retrieved_chunks.title: Sub-Chapter 2. Veterans
retrieved_chunks.content:
a. Veterans are eligible for Post-9/11 GI Bill Education Benefits if they served a minimum of 90 days on active duty after September 10, 2001, or at least 30 days if discharged for a service-connected disability or sole survivorship. This covers certain types of active duty served as a member of the Uniformed Services or because of a call up or mobilization to active duty from a reserve component (National Guard or Selected Reserve) under certain sections of title 10, title 14, or title 32.

Basic entry-level and skills training does not count towards qualifying service unless the Veteran has completed 730 days (24 months) of service. For more information, see 70/80 rule below.

Note: 38 USC § 3301 and 38 CFR § 21.9505 specifically use the term “Armed Forces” when referencing qualifying active duty service, rather than “Uniformed Services”. VA General Counsel has determined the two terms have the same meaning for purposes of VA benefits due to 33 USC 3072(a) and 42 USC 213(d), stating service in the National Oceanic and Atmospheric Administration and Public Health Service, respectively, is considered active military service under laws administered by the Secretary of Veterans Affairs. Therefore, the reference to “Armed Forces” in 38 USC § 3301 should be treated as having the same meaning as “Uniformed Services”.

b. Veterans

To qualify for chapter 33 benefits, a Veteran must have served at least 90 aggregate (total) days of active duty service after September 10, 2001, and have an honorable discharge. Three years of total service or a discharge due to a service-connected disability or sole survivorship will qualify a Veteran for 100% of the Post-9/11 GI Bill benefit. Less than 3 years of cumulative service will qualify a Veteran for a lower percentage of benefits.

c. National Guard/Reserve

National Guard members and reservists who have served a minimum of 90 days of qualifying service are eligible to receive chapter 33 benefits.

Note: Effective October 1, 2011, VA began paying benefits to members of the Army National Guard and Air National Guard based on qualifying service under title 32. Payments may be made retroactively for enrollment(s) at an approved educational institution for attendance on or after August 1, 2009. The following service may be qualifying:

results.answer:
Based on the provided context, avocational or recreational courses are **presumed to be precluded** under GI Bill benefits unless the veteran can establish that the course will be of **"bona fide use in the pursuit of the business or occupation."** [1]

Specifically, courses presumed to be avocational or recreational include:

- Photography or entertainment courses
- Music courses (instrument or vocal)
- Public speaking courses
- Dancing, sports, or athletics courses (e.g., horseback riding, swimming, fishing, skiing, golf, baseball, tennis, bowling, sports officiating)
- Any other course determined to be avocational or recreational [1]

**Exceptions exist** for courses such as applied music, physical education, or public speaking that are offered by Institutions of Higher Learning (IHLs) **for credit as an integral part of a program leading to an educational objective.** [1]

To overcome the avocational/recreational presumption, a veteran may provide evidence such as:

1. **A statement on the application** explaining the business or occupational objective
2. **A statement from someone in the business** or from an employer showing the need and use of the course [1]

In short, these courses are not automatically approved — the burden is on the veteran to demonstrate legitimate occupational relevance.

results.query: Can VA pay for a private pilot's certificate course at a flight school?
results.expected_answer: No. Vocational flight training for a private pilot's certificate is always prohibited. However, it may be taken at an IHL if it is for credit toward a college degree.

retrieved_chunks.content:
For general requirements and procedures, see Section 4.06.

1. The flight charges certified for reimbursement on the VA Form 22-6553c must be in accordance with the rates approved by the SAA, or by VA in those states where the regional office acts as the SAA. Payment is based on reimbursement of 60 percent of the approved charges for flight training pursued by eligible beneficiaries. Reimbursement is restricted to the charges for solo and dual flight instruction, ground school instruction, and pre- and postflight instruction associated with solo and dual flight training hours.

2. Eligible VA students must pay 40 percent of the approved charges for the training received.

3. The flight training courses listed below contain required solo flight training time. Other flight training courses may also include solo flight training if it is included in the training course outline or training program curriculum.

i. Commercial Pilot Certificate

ii. Commercial Pilot (Helicopter)

iii. Commercial Pilot (Gliders)

iv. Commercial Pilot (Free Balloon)

v. Flight Instructor (limited to "pilot-in-command" time)

vi. Commercial Test Pilot

4. Students not receiving VA benefits who are used in the 85 percent enrollment computation must pay 100 percent of the approved charges for instruction. The course may not be offered at reduced rates to such students not receiving VA benefits unless it is also offered to VA students at the same reduced rates and on the same basis, e.g., discounted rates for payment in advance. Employee discounts must be provided  on an equal basis to VA students and students not receiving VA benefits.

heading_path: M22-4, Part 10, Compliance Survey. Chapter 04, Procedures Applicable to Facilities Offering Specific Types of Training.
SUBCHAPTER 03. AREAS OF REVIEW FOR FLIGHT SCHOOLS
4.35 POSSESSION OF VALID PRIVATE PILOT CERTIFICATE OR HIGHER RATING AND APPROPRIATE MEDICAL CERTIFICATE
retrieved_chunks.content:
1. A veteran, service member, or reservist who has basic eligibility to receive educational assistance allowance under the provisions of chapters 30, 32, or 1606, may receive educational assistance for flight training provided that the student also possesses an unlimited private pilot certificate issued pursuant to FAR part 61, subpart E, or higher certificate or rating before enrolling in the flight training course. Benefits are not payable if the individual possesses only a student pilot or recreational pilot certificate before entry into the approved flight training course. Recreational certificates do not meet the private pilot certificate requirement. For service members on flight status, a commercial pilot certificate (fixed wing or rotor craft) will satisfy this requirement. In order to show that the student meets this requirement, the school must maintain in its records a photocopy of the student's private pilot certificate or higher rating or certificate.

2. The student must also meet the medical requirements for the flight course being pursued. The medical requirements for the course must be met at the time of initial enrollment and continuously during pursuit of the flight training course. For all flight training courses except the ATP certificate, the student must possess a valid 2nd class medical certificate. For the ATP course, the student must possess a valid 1st class medical certificate.

3. A 1st class medical certificate is valid until the last day of the sixth month after the month of medical examination. The certificate then reverts to a 2nd class medical certificate, which is only valid for six additional months, because the expiration date is determined as stated in subparagraph (4) below.

4. A 2nd class medical certificate is valid until the last day of the 12th month after the month of medical examination.  The certificate then reverts to a 3rd class medical certificate.

EXAMPLES:

A 1st class medical certificate based upon a medical examination conducted on January 15, 1996, expires for ATP training on July 31, 1996. The certificate reverts to a 2nd class medical certificate, acceptable for all other flight training. The 2nd class medical certificate expires on January 31, 1997. The certificate reverts to a 3rd class medical certificate, valid only for private pilot operations, and not acceptable for flight training.

A 2nd class medical certificate based upon a medical examination conducted on January 15, 1996, expires on January 31, 1997. The certificate reverts to a 3rd class medical certificate, valid only for private pilot operations, and not acceptable for flight training.

6. Education benefits will not be paid for pursuit of a flight objective for which the student is already qualified. (38 USC 3471; 38 CFR 21.4230(c)*) A student who has obtained a flight certificate or rating for a course, or who could obtain that certificate or rating without further training, is already qualified.

i. Former military pilots with the equivalent of a commercial pilot certificate and instrument rating may obtain an FAA commercial pilot certificate and instrument rating without the FAA flight examination within 12 months after release from active duty flying status. Refresher training in commercial or instrument courses within 12 months after active duty flying status is not permitted.

ii. Refresher training may be provided for veterans who once held an FAA certificate and the certificate was canceled or surrendered before or during the veteran's qualifying service. In such cases, the refresher training must update the veteran's knowledge and skills in the technological advances made during and since the veteran's active military service.

An individual who held a FAA certificate before or during active duty service may have surrendered that certificate or the FAA may have canceled it. The individual may receive the equivalent of the number of months of educational assistance necessary to complete the course that will qualify him or her for the same grade certificate. 

 Institution of Higher Learning (IHL) Flight

1.  IHLs offering flight training must maintain a record of VA student’s previous education and training, grant appropriate credit for previous education and training, shorten the student's course proportionately, and notify the student and the VA of any course changes or modifications. (38 CFR 21.4253(d)(3); 21.4254(c)(4)).

1. A veteran, service member, or reservist who has basic eligibility to receive educational assistance allowance under the provisions of chapters 30, 32, or 1606, may receive educational assistance for flight training provided that the student also possesses an unlimited private pilot certificate issued pursuant to FAR part 61, subpart E, or higher certificate or rating before enrolling in the flight training course. Benefits are not payable if the individual possesses only a student pilot or recreational pilot certificate before entry into the approved flight training course. Recreational certificates do not meet the private pilot certificate requirement. For service members on flight status, a commercial pilot certificate (fixed wing or rotor craft) will satisfy this requirement. In order to show that the student meets this requirement, the school must maintain in its records a photocopy of the student's private pilot certificate or higher rating or certificate.

2. A 1st class medical certificate is valid until the last day of the sixth month after the month of medical examination. The certificate then reverts to a 2nd class medical certificate, which is only valid for six additional months, because the expiration date is determined as stated in subparagraph (4) below.

ii. Refresher training may be provided for veterans who once held an FAA certificate and the certificate was canceled or surrendered before or during the veteran's qualifying service. In such cases, the refresher training must update the veteran's knowledge and skills in the technological advances made during and since the veteran's active military service.

results.answer:
Based on the provided context, **no, VA cannot pay for a private pilot's certificate course at a flight school** under chapters 30, 32, or 1606.

Specifically, the requirements state that a veteran, service member, or reservist **must already possess** an unlimited private pilot certificate (or higher certificate or rating) issued pursuant to FAR part 61, subpart E **before enrolling** in a flight training course in order to receive educational assistance. [4]

In other words, the private pilot certificate is a **prerequisite** for receiving VA flight training benefits — it is not itself a course VA will pay for. Additionally, benefits are not payable if the individual possesses only a student pilot or recreational pilot certificate prior to entry into an approved flight training course. [4]

2.10  COMBINATION COLLEGE DEGREE – FLIGHT TRAINING PROGRAMS
retrieved_chunks.content:
a. College Degrees.

1. All students must have a  second-class medical certificate prior to the approval and start of any flight training program, unless the training is conducted under FAR Part 61.  Students do not have to take flight courses in the order specified in subparagraph 2.03a (2).

2. A student may receive benefits for private pilot certification provided the course is listed under 14 CFR Part 141 as an approved part of the standard college degree the student is pursing, or is listed as in house 14 CFR Part 61 training and approved by the State Approving Agency (SAA).

3. Flight schools and flight training centers, under contract with colleges or universities, may only provide flight training for all programs above private pilot and must be identified on their Part 141 Air Agency Certificate

4. The degree requirements must specifically identify the required number flight hours for each flight course in the school catalog. Training beyond the degree requirements is vocational.  Students under chapters 30, 32, 33 and 1606, may interrupt their college program and receive reimbursement for flight training above the private pilot level at a flight school or flight training center.  The student may return to college training without being charged with a change of program if they meet all of the following conditions:

[i.] The flight training pursued at the flight school or flight training center does not exceed the amount required for the degree.

[ii.] The college or university grants full credit for the flight course(s) in partial fulfillment of the degree requirements.

[iii.] Students continue to pursue the same degree programs.

[NOTE: A first-class medical certificate is valid for six months for operations requiring an airline transport pilot certificate. It is valid for twelve months for operations requiring other flight certificates. In essence a first class medical certificate is valid for six months; then it becomes a second class medical certificate for six months (14 CFR, Sec. 61.23(d)(1)).
 
1. If a student is enrolled in a flight course other than an Airline Transport Pilot (ATP) course, the student must hold a second-class medical certificate on the first day of training. (If that course began before October 1, 1998, the student must have held that certificate continuously during training.) A second-class medical certificate expires at the end of the last day of the twelfth month following the date of examination shown on the certificate ((14 CFR, Sec. 61.23(d)). (See Policy Advisory dated 10/11/05)

2. If a student is enrolled in an ATP certification course, the student must hold a first-class medical certificate on the first day of training. A first-class medical certificate expires at the end of the last day of the sixth month following the date of examination shown on the certificate (14 CFR, Sec. 61.23(d)).
 
Example: A first-class medical certificate issued on June 12, 2004, is valid through December 31, 2004.]
 
3. A student in a ground instructor course does not need to have a medical certificate.

retrieved_chunks.content:
1. [Certifications as to Medical Certificates. For the initial enrollment certification for a flight course, flight schools should put the name and date of the medical certificate in the Remarks section of the enrollment certification. If this medical certificate information is not submitted, RPOs will develop for it. If the medical certificate is not valid on the day that the course begins, RPOs will deny the claim.
 

NOTE: If the Enrollment Certification is accompanied by VA Form 22-6553c, Monthly Certification of Flight Training, the adjudicator should accept the certification shown on this form as to the student's medical certificate.

2. Questions and Answers

Question 1: If the claimant is enrolled in consecutive flight courses at the same flight school (CFI, CFII, etc.), do we need medical certificate information each time?

Answer 1: No, medical certificate information should not be requested each time a claimant is enrolled in consecutive flight courses when the claimant has a valid medical certificate covering the beginning date of each course in question.

Example: Jack received his second class medical certificate which was dated September 1, 2002. He starts a course on October 2, 2002, and completes the course on November 5, 2002, and begins a new course on November 7, 2002. Since a second class medical certificate is good for 12 months, we could pay benefits during the consecutive enrollments.

Question 2: A veteran began flight training on October 1, 2002. Although he had an expired 2nd class medical certificate, this was overlooked by him and the school certifying official. As a result, the veteran can not be paid for any training. However, if the veteran quits the course, gets the proper medical certificate and reenrolls in the course, can he began receiving benefits?

Answer 2: Yes, the veteran can begin receiving benefits if he gets the proper medical certificate and reenrolls in the course. Although we can not pay him for the portion of the course already completed, we can grant appropriate credit.]

results.answer:
## Medical Certificate Requirement for Non-ATP Flight Courses

For any flight training course **other than the Airline Transport Pilot (ATP) certificate**, a student must possess a valid **2nd class medical certificate** [1][4].

### Key Requirements:

- The valid 2nd class medical certificate must be held **on the first day of training** [4]
- A 2nd class medical certificate is valid until the **last day of the 12th month** after the month of medical examination [1][4]

> **Example:** A second-class medical certificate issued on April 9, 2003, is valid through April 30, 2004. [4]

### Additional Notes:
- Flight schools certify on **VA Form 22-1999** that the student has the required medical certificate [4]
- For the initial enrollment certification, flight schools should include the **name and date of the medical certificate** in the Remarks section [5]
- If the medical certificate is **not valid on the day the course begins**, the RPO will deny the claim [5]

> **Contrast:** For the ATP course specifically, a **1st class medical certificate** is required on the first day of training [1][4].

SUBCHAPTER III, MEASURING ENROLLMENTS WITH SPECIAL TYPES OF COURSES
retrieved_chunks.content:
a.  Background

Practice teaching (or student teaching) often requires a full day in the classroom, 5 days a week. 

However, the number of semester or quarter hours of credit granted for this training varies and is sometimes not commensurate with the amount of time actually expended.

b.  School certification

The enrollment certification for students enrolled in practice teaching should indicate that they are pursuing practice-teaching.

Schools may report both credit hours and clock hours on the enrollment certification.

c.  Principle

The training time for students in practice-teaching will be measured on either a clock-hour or a credit-hour basis, whichever will provide the greater benefit.

NOTE: Eighteen clock hours per week will be considered full time for an accredited course in practice teaching when the course is measured on a clock-hour basis.  See chapter 7 for clock-hour measurement tables.

d.  Example

A student is enrolled in practice teaching for 18 clock hours and 9 credit hours.  Eighteen clock hours is full time whereas 9 credit hours is 3/4 time.  Measure this training on a clock-hour basis since it will provide the greater benefit.

retrieved_chunks.title: 1607 Part 11: Chapter 6 - Payment and Authorization Processes
heading_path: 1607 Part 11: Chapter 6 - Payment and Authorization Processes
retrieved_chunks.content:
NOTE: BDN will not correctly calculate the rate in all cases for servicepersons at any training time and veterans at less than 1/'2 time if the individual has a chapter 1606 kicker.

When calculating the monthly rate due a student based on training time or duty status, first determine the Chapter 1607 statutory rate due and then add in the proportional kicker based on training time. If training time is less than 1/2 time but 1/4 time or more, pay 1/4 of the Chapter 1606 kicker. If BDN is not paying the rate correctly, override the rate on the 312 screen with an "M" override.

In all three examples below the term is 1/6/06 through 5/12/06 (5/13 no pay date), 1/4 time training (training time 0), 1607 is payable at the 60% rate, and the claimant is entitled to a $350.00 1606 kicker. For a student in the 60% category the maximum monthly rate payable for less than half-time training (effective 10/1/05) is $310.20.

Example 1: Tuition and fees are 50.00. The calculated monthly rate is $11.81 and one quarter of the 1606 kicker is $87.50. Currently BDN would pay only the $11.81, however the total award due the claimant is $99.31 (11.81 + 87.50). Use the M override on this award to pay $99.31.

Example 2: Tuition and fees are $1500.00. The calculated monthly rate is $354.33 to fully reimburse tuition and fees; BDN included a portion of the kicker ($44.13) to reach the rate necessary to reimburse the student fully. In reality, the total award due the claimant is $397.70 ($310.20 + 87.50). Use the M override on this award to pay $397.70.

Example 3: Tuition and fees are $5000.00. The calculated monthly rate is $1181.10 to fully reimburse tuition and fees, BDN will cap the rate at $310.20 and include the full $87.50 kicker in order to pay the maximum amount payable to the student $310.20 + $87.50). There is no need to use the M override in this scenario, we are paying the maximum amount payable.

In cases in which the student is due a lump sum award, and BDN is not paying the full amount due, override the monthly rate displayed on the 312 screen using the "M" override. Unfortunately, the student will not receive a lump sum award in this instance, but will receive the 1606 kicker included in his payment each month.

 Go to Top

Based on the provided context, a summer-term interval that exceeds 30 days is **not payable**. The context does not describe any exception that would make such an interval payable.

What the context does establish is that for summer-term interval payment, **two conditions must both be met**:

1. The interval must be **less than a full calendar month** (i.e., not exceeding 30 days), AND
2. The summer term must be **at least 8 weeks long** [1][2]

For example, a student is explicitly described as **not eligible** for a summer-fall interval payment because "that interval is more than 30 days, although less than a full calendar month, and the summer term is less than 8 weeks" [1] — suggesting the 30-day threshold is a firm restriction regardless of term length.

The context does not provide any scenario where an interval exceeding 30 days would still be payable for a summer term. If there are additional exceptions, they are not contained in the provided context.

retrieved_chunks.content:
(1)  Designate the folder for the second spouse as XXX-XX-XXXX - W(2).  XXX-XX-XXXX stands for the folder number (the   same number as the veteran's file number).  "W(2)" represents the alpha designation for the second spouse.  "W(3)" would be the alpha designation for the third spouse.  Annotate the inactive folder to show that a different spouse is using the same suffix.  Store the  previous (inactive) DEA folder in inactive storage.

(2)  If a suitable child's record is located as described in subparagraph b above, follow the procedures in subparagraph d to establish this record for the new payee.  If the previous DEA folder number was XXX-XX-XXXX - A, designate the new folder as XXX-XX-XXXX - A (2).  Annotate the inactive folder to show that another child is using the same suffix.  Store the previous (inactive) DEA   folder in inactive storage.

(3)  If a suitable child's record is not located, designate the DEA folder for the tenth child as XXX-XX-XXXX - J.  "J" represents the alpha designation for the tenth child.  "K" would be the designation for the eleventh child.  Out-of-system processing is required for these payees (see subpar. f).

retrieved_chunks.title: Part 3: Chapter 1 - Administrative Issues
retrieved_chunks.content:
(1) Electronic Folder Management System. All Education processing is done within TIMS. This is the primary folder for storing claims related information. For additional information regarding creating folders in TIMS, refer to Chapter 14, Upstream Procedures.

NOTE: The only types of folders that can be recorded in BDN (Benefit Delivery Network) are claims folders, Chapter 30 folders, DEA (Dependents’ Educational Assistance) folders, and EDU (Education) folders. BDN does not indicate whether a TIMS folder or a paper folder exists.

(2) Claims Folder. This is a three-part red paper folder known as red-rope. It has three flaps with fasteners attached on the inside. Claims folders were used to process Chapter 32, Section 903, and Chapter 1606 benefits before June 1994. Since June 1994, Education (EDU) folders were used for these benefits. The Waco RO used paper claims folders for Section 901 claims until the Muskogee RPO assumed jurisdiction for this benefit in 2005. Since then, Section 901 records have been converted to TIMS. The Chapter 34 benefit of Title 38 (which expired on December 31, 1989) was processed entirely with claims folders.

(3) CH30 (Chapter 30) Folder. This is a two-part paper folder containing Chapter 30 material only. Initially, RPOs built two-part gray folders for Chapter 30 claims until the stock of those folders was depleted. Then the RPOs used Kraft folders (light brown in color) as Chapter 30 folders. These folders were discontinued in the 1990s and replaced with EDU (Education) folders.

(4) EDU Folder. This is a two-part Kraft brown paper folder containing material for Chapters 30, 32, 1606, or Section 903. The physical EDU folders have not been created since the 1990s. However, the TIMS folders for all education benefits (except Chapter 35) are recorded as EDU folders in BDN.

NOTE 1: For claims established before October 16, 2000, a Veteran could have two folders (both an EDU and a CH30 folder) shown in BDN and in some instances two paper folders. In this situation, RPOs had two options: to retain both folders, or to destroy one folder and retain only one folder. If the RPO decided to destroy a folder, the RPO needed to request and adjustment in BDN from the Records Management Center (RMC). A claimant should only have one electronic folder.

NOTE 2: The multiple folders in BIRLS (Benefits Information Record Location System) or BDN occurred because before October 1, 2000, the CEST (Claims Establishment) processing for Chapter 30 caused BIRLS to indicate the existence of a CH30 folder and the CEST processing for Chapters 32 and 1606 caused BIRLS to indicate the existence of an EDU folder. CEST processing was changed effective October 1, 2000, to automatically create only an EDU folder in BIRLS for these education benefits.

(5) Dependents' Educational Assistance (DEA) Folder. This is a two-part blue paper folder created for Chapter 35 benefits. These paper folders are seldom used except in rare situations when a dependent has no SSN (Social Security Number), there is a second (subsequent) spouse, or a 10th (subsequent) child and TIMS is unable to support the paperless folder.

6.08  MATURING PENDING ISSUES - W50 SCREEN

There are two purposes for the Maturing Pending Issues screen. Column A identifies issues on which action is due, to manage the workload and assure timely processing of claims. Columns B through G identify issues on which action is past due, to identify problem areas and help resolve out-of-line situations in particular end products. The time categories for issues on the W50 screen are based on the suspense date, not the date of claim. This suspense date is automatically generated during CEST (Claims ESTablishment) and CADJ processing to establish a new pending issue, or when a future diary issue matures. For education end products, the automatic suspense date is 30 days in the future. However, for authorization issues, the generated date may be modified by the manual entry of a desired suspense date on the claims establishment screen during CEST, CADJ, or PCHG (Pending issues CHanGe) processing, or on the S99 screen when accessed by the CDEV, CDAT, or CADJ commands. For ESU issues, this can only be done by canceling the issue using WCAN, and re-establishing it using WEST (Work ESTablishment) with the new date. See Figure 6.7 for a representation of the W50 screen layout.

Sub-Chapter 5. Eligibility Excluded Service

Certain periods of active-duty service are not qualifying for the purposes of establishing eligibility for chapter 33 benefits. For example, periods of service obligated by a Loan Repayment Program (LRP), or a commission as a result of a ROTC program under Title 10 USC 2107 (b), or graduation from a Service Academy do not qualify as active duty. 3.16  Service Academy Preparatory Schools and Service Academies
retrieved_chunks.content:
EXAMPLE: A West Point student graduates and is commissioned on May 27, 2003, and served through May 26, 2008. The individual's academy commitment ended on May 26, 2008. The five year excluded period runs from May 27, 2003 through May 26, 2008. The first date eligibility for a period of qualifying service may be counted is May 27, 2008.

Special Rule for Cadets of the Coast Guard Academy: The requirement under Chapter 33 to exclude the first five years of active duty service following commission should not apply to Coast Guard Academy graduates of the Class of 2014 or earlier. In general, the requirement to serve on active duty for five years immediately after appointment prevents Service Academy Graduates from accruing creditable service towards the Post-9/11 GI Bill until the obligation period has been served. This provision will now also apply to former cadets of the Coast Guard Academy graduating in the Class of 2015 or later beginning upon their date of commission (the only way for exemption from the five year exclusion is if the person was originally a member of the Class of 2014 or earlier but, for some reason, ultimately graduated with a later class). This is due to the fact that the Coast Guard Academy Cadet pursuant to a commitment signed after January 4, 2011, is obligating service after the legislation became law and effective.

See  Part 12, Chapter 00: Index for information regarding Chapter 33, Information about Benefits, Excluded Service.

See Part 5 Chapter 1 - General for the effect of graduating from a service academy on Chapter 30 eligibility.

In general, the requirement to serve on active duty for five years immediately after appointment prevents Service Academy Graduates from accruing creditable service towards the Post-9/11 GI Bill until the obligation period has been served. This provision will now also apply to former cadets of the Coast Guard Academy graduating in the Class of 2015 or later beginning upon their date of commission (the only way for exemption from the five year exclusion is if the person was originally a member of the Class of 2014 or earlier but, for some reason, ultimately graduated with a later class). This is due to the fact that the Coast Guard Academy Cadet pursuant to a commitment signed after January 4, 2011, is obligating service after the legislation became law and effective.

results.expected_answer: 60% of established tuition and fees for one term

retrieved_chunks.content:
If the tuition and fees do not exceed 200 percent of the total basic educational assistance otherwise payable, the spreadsheet will display the message “Not Eligible for Accelerated Payment.”  Pay regular chapter 30 benefits and send the claimant a disallowance letter (see PCGL DIS-20).

If the total charges exceed 200 percent of the total basic educational assistance otherwise payable, the spreadsheet will display one of these two messages:

heading_path: 11.06 RESERVED(Change Date:  April 28, 2008)11.06 RESERVED(Change Date:  April 28, 2008)
retrieved_chunks.content:
EXAMPLE 1:  This example is limited to the issue of what costs are payable.  A school offers an IT certification Boot Camp program without any discounts. The school's catalog says, "The total cost of the program billed to students is $6,995." The $6995 includes:

Fees
 Amount
Registration
$100
Courseware and materials
$500
Lab Fees
$225
Practice Exams
$150
Exam Fees
$1000
Total Fees
$1975
Tuition
$4,020
Total Tuition & Fees $5995
Lodging & Airfare $1000
Total Cost of Program $6995

For accelerated payment purposes, the established tuition and fees (and the actual tuition and fees) would be $4,345. This is calculated by subtracting from the total cost of the program ($6,995) the lodging & airfare, the courseware and materials, practice exams and exam fees. The accelerated payment would be 60 percent of $4345 or $2,607. The student might be eligible for reimbursement of 100 percent of the $1,000 of certification tests as well under the licensing and certification benefit. 

NOTE:  For non-accelerated payment purposes, VA has not previously allowed institutions to include dormitory fees or the cost of travel to the college or books, courseware, practice exams, or certification exam fees as part of the fees for a course.

EXAMPLE 2:  A veteran enrolls in a full-time high tech program. The actual tuition and fees and the established tuition and fees are both $15,000 and the length of the program is 4 months.  The regular chapter 30 full-time rate is $900 per month.  The monthly rate based on the tuition and fees is $3,750, which is more than 200 percent of $900.  Therefore, the student qualifies for accelerated payment. In this case, the student will receive 60 percent of $15,000 for a payment of $9,000. 

EXAMPLE 3:  A veteran enrolls in a full-time high tech program.  The actual tuition and fees and the established tuition and fees are both $10,000 and the length of the program is 10 months.  The regular chapter 30 full-time rate is $900 per month.  The monthly rate based on the tuition and fees is $1,000, which is less than 200 percent of $900.  Therefore, the student does not qualify for accelerated payment. 

EXAMPLE 4:  Established tuition and fees for a high tech program are $12,000.  The length of the enrollment period is 3 months at full-time training.  Actual tuition and fees to be paid by the student after the discount is $7,200. The regular full-time training rate is $900.  The monthly rate based on the actual tuition and fees is $2,400, which is more than 200 percent of $900.  Therefore, the claimant is entitled to accelerated payment of $7,200 (60 percent of $12,000).

NOTE:  The 60 percent calculation is based on the established tuition and fees, not the actual tuition and fees.  In this particular case, the claimant would be entitled to 100 percent of actual tuition and fees. 

EXAMPLE 5:  Established tuition and fees for the high tech program are $12,000 and the length of the enrollment period is 4 months of full-time training.  Actual tuition and fees paid by the student will be $7,200.  The regular full-time training rate is $900.  The monthly rate based on the actual tuition and fees is $1,800, which is not more than 200 percent of $900.  (It is exactly 200 percent.)  This student would not be eligible for accelerated payment because the fees do not pass the 200 percent test.

retrieved_chunks.title: Part 5: Chapter 11 - Accelerated Payment
retrieved_chunks.content:
An accelerated payment is a lump sum payment of a maximum of 60 percent of the established tuition and fees for an individual's enrollment for a term, quarter, or semester in an approved program of education leading to employment in a high technology industry.  In the case of a program of education not offered on a term, quarter, or semester basis, the accelerated payment is a lump sum payment of a maximum of 60 percent of the established tuition and fees for the entire program (38 CFR 21.7020(b)(51)). 

NOTE 1:  There is no provision for paying less than 60 percent of tuition and fees based on a claimant's request. VA would only pay less than 60 percent if the claimant had insufficient remaining entitlement or reached his or her delimiting date during the enrollment period for which accelerated payment was requested.  

NOTE 2:  Accelerated payment is a completely separate benefit from lump sum payment (see pt. V, par. 1.25 for lump sum payment.)  

Go to Top

retrieved_chunks.content:
